era_context: Continuation of the late Archaic period in Greece; Lydia under Alyattes II expanding militarily; the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nabopolassar in its early consolidation phase; the Spring and Autumn period in China with increasing interstate conflict; the Levant experiencing Phoenician and Neo-Assyrian influence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: India (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Continued Lydian military campaigns in Anatolia
events.description: Alyattes II maintains military pressure on neighboring territories and Greek city-states as part of Lydia's imperial expansion. The Lydian state is consolidating control over central Anatolia and establishing itself as a major regional power. These campaigns are part of a broader pattern of Lydian expansion that would continue through the 620s–600s BCE.
events.certainty_note: No specific event is attested for 633 BCE. This entry reflects the general pattern of Lydian military activity during Alyattes' reign, which spanned several decades. The year is assigned by convention within the broader campaign period.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are reliably attested for 633 BCE. The entry above is a generalization based on the broader pattern of Alyattes' reign. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether Herodotus' account of individual campaigns can be dated to specific years.
historiographic_note: For 633 BCE, the historical record is extremely thin. No contemporary sources directly attest to events in this specific year. The entry relies on inference from broader historical patterns and later compilations. This year exemplifies the challenge of year-by-year history in the ancient world—most sources group events by reign or generation rather than by individual years.
